name: Linux Server Monitoring
author: OpsDB Platform
description: Monitor Linux servers (Ubuntu 20.04+ / systemd) via SSH: CPU, memory, disk I/O, network, systemd services, processes, journald logs, and security events. Collects from /proc, ss, ps, df, and journalctl. Provides real-time performance metrics, service health, event log error tracking, and security audit monitoring.

setup_requirements.title: SSH Daemon Running
setup_requirements.description: OpenSSH server (sshd) must be installed and running. OpsDB connects via SSH to execute monitoring commands.

setup_requirements.title: Firewall Port 22 Open (SSH)
setup_requirements.description: The OpsDB backend must be able to reach TCP port 22 (or your configured SSH port) on the target server.

setup_requirements.title: Valid SSH Username & Password/Key
setup_requirements.description: The username and password (or private key) stored as a Credential in OpsDB must be valid for this host. For key auth, the public key must be in ~/.ssh/authorized_keys for that user.

setup_requirements.title: Python 3.6 or Later Installed
setup_requirements.description: All collectors run as Python 3 one-liners over SSH. Python 3.6+ is required.

setup_requirements.title: /proc and /sys Filesystems Mounted
setup_requirements.description: CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network collectors read from /proc and /proc/net/dev. These virtual filesystems are always present on any running Linux kernel.

setup_requirements.title: systemd (for Service Monitoring)
setup_requirements.description: The linux.services_health and linux.services_details collectors require systemctl. Ubuntu 16.04+ uses systemd by default. Without it, those two collectors return zeros instead of failing.

setup_requirements.title: journalctl Access (for Log Collectors)
setup_requirements.description: The linux.syslog_errors, linux.journal_errors, and linux.failed_logins collectors use journalctl. The SSH user must be in the systemd-journal group or have sudo access.

setup_requirements.title: ss (Socket Statistics) Command
setup_requirements.description: Network and TCP connection collectors use ss from the iproute2 package.

linux.system_uptime.description: Server uptime in seconds, 1/5/15-minute load averages, and running/total process counts from /proc/uptime and /proc/loadavg. Run every minute to detect unexpected reboots.

linux.cpu_utilization.description: CPU usage percentage (1-second differential from /proc/stat), plus 1/5/15-minute load averages and logical CPU core count.
linux.cpu_utilization.command:
python3 -c "import json,time
s1=open('/proc/stat').readline().split()
i1,t1=int(s1[4]),sum(int(x) for x in s1[1:])
time.sleep(1)
s2=open('/proc/stat').readline().split()
i2,t2=int(s2[4]),sum(int(x) for x in s2[1:])
dt=t2-t1
cpu=round((1-(i2-i1)/dt)*100,1) if dt else 0.0
la=open('/proc/loadavg').read().split()
cores=sum(1 for l in open('/proc/cpuinfo') if l.startswith('processor'))
print(json.dumps({'cpu_usage_percent':cpu,'load_avg_1':float(la[0]),'load_avg_5':float(la[1]),'load_avg_15':float(la[2]),'cpu_cores':cores}))"

linux.memory_utilization.description: Memory usage percentage, used/free/available/total bytes, buffer and cache bytes, swap usage percentage and bytes. All from /proc/meminfo.
linux.memory_utilization.command: python3 -c "import json;m={l.split(':')[0].strip():int(l.split()[1])*1024 for l in open('/proc/meminfo') if ':' in l and len(l.split())>=2 and l.split()[1].isdigit()};t=m.get('MemTotal',1);a=m.get('MemAvailable',m.get('MemFree',0));u=t-a;st=m.get('SwapTotal',0);sf=m.get('SwapFree',0);su=st-sf;print(json.dumps({'memory_used_percent':round(u/t*100,1),'memory_used_bytes':u,'memory_available_bytes':a,'memory_total_bytes':t,'memory_free_bytes':m.get('MemFree',0),'cached_bytes':m.get('Cached',0),'buffers_bytes':m.get('Buffers',0),'swap_used_percent':round(su/st*100,1) if st else 0.0,'swap_used_bytes':su,'swap_total_bytes':st}))"

linux.disk_utilization.description: Aggregate disk usage across all real filesystems: volume count, maximum used percentage, total/free/used bytes. Excludes /proc, /sys, /run, and snap mounts.
linux.disk_utilization.command: python3 -c "import json,subprocess;lines=subprocess.check_output(['df','-B1','--output=source,size,used,avail,pcent,target']).decode().strip().split('\n')[1:];vols=[l.split() for l in lines];vols=[v for v in vols if len(v)==6 and v[5].startswith('/') and not any(v[5].startswith(x) for x in ['/proc','/sys','/dev/shm','/run','/snap'])];total=sum(int(v[1]) for v in vols);free=sum(int(v[3]) for v in vols);used=sum(int(v[2]) for v in vols);pcts=[int(v[4].rstrip('%')) for v in vols];print(json.dumps({'volume_count':len(vols),'max_disk_used_pct':max(pcts) if pcts else 0,'total_disk_bytes':total,'free_disk_bytes':free,'used_disk_bytes':used}))"

linux.disk_details.description: Per-filesystem disk space breakdown: device, mount point, total/used/free bytes, and usage percentage. Returns one row per mounted real filesystem.
linux.disk_details.command: python3 -c "import json,subprocess;lines=subprocess.check_output(['df','-B1','--output=source,size,used,avail,pcent,target']).decode().strip().split('\n')[1:];rows=[];[rows.append({'device':p[0],'total_bytes':int(p[1]),'used_bytes':int(p[2]),'free_bytes':int(p[3]),'used_pct':int(p[4].rstrip('%')),'mount':p[5]}) for l in lines for p in [l.split()] if len(p)==6 and p[5].startswith('/') and not any(p[5].startswith(x) for x in ['/proc','/sys','/dev/shm','/run','/snap'])];print(json.dumps(rows if rows else [{'device':'none','total_bytes':0,'used_bytes':0,'free_bytes':0,'used_pct':0,'mount':'/'}]))"

linux.disk_io.description: Block device I/O throughput and IOPS: read/write bytes per second and read/write operations per second (1-second differential from /proc/diskstats). Covers sd*, vd*, nvme*, and md* devices.
linux.disk_io.command:
python3 -c "import json,time
def rd():
    rc=rb=wc=wb=0
    for l in open('/proc/diskstats'):
        p=l.split()
        if len(p)>=13 and not p[2].startswith('loop'):
            d=p[2]
            if d[:2] in ('sd','vd','hd','md') or d.startswith(('nvme','xvd')):
                rc+=int(p[3]);rb+=int(p[5])*512;wc+=int(p[7]);wb+=int(p[9])*512
    return rc,rb,wc,wb
rc1,rb1,wc1,wb1=rd()
time.sleep(1)
rc2,rb2,wc2,wb2=rd()
print(json.dumps({'disk_read_bytes_per_sec':max(0,rb2-rb1),'disk_write_bytes_per_sec':max(0,wb2-wb1),'read_iops':max(0,rc2-rc1),'write_iops':max(0,wc2-wc1)}))"

linux.network_io.description: Aggregate network throughput across non-loopback interfaces: bytes received/sent per second and cumulative receive/send error counts (1-second differential from /proc/net/dev).
linux.network_io.command:
python3 -c "import json,time
def rn():
    r={}
    for l in open('/proc/net/dev').readlines()[2:]:
        if ':' in l:
            i,d=l.split(':',1)
            i=i.strip()
            if i!='lo':
                v=d.split()
                r[i]=(int(v[0]),int(v[8]),int(v[2]),int(v[10]))
    return r
n1=rn()
time.sleep(1)
n2=rn()
rx=sum(max(0,n2[i][0]-n1[i][0]) for i in n2 if i in n1)
tx=sum(max(0,n2[i][1]-n1[i][1]) for i in n2 if i in n1)
rex=sum(n2[i][2] for i in n2)
tex=sum(n2[i][3] for i in n2)
print(json.dumps({'bytes_received_per_sec':rx,'bytes_sent_per_sec':tx,'total_receive_errors':rex,'total_send_errors':tex}))"

linux.network_interfaces.description: Per-interface cumulative network statistics: bytes and packets received/sent, errors, and drops. Returns one row per non-loopback interface from /proc/net/dev.
linux.network_interfaces.command: python3 -c "import json;rows=[];[rows.append({'interface':i.strip(),'rx_bytes':int(v[0]),'rx_packets':int(v[1]),'rx_errors':int(v[2]),'rx_drop':int(v[3]),'tx_bytes':int(v[8]),'tx_packets':int(v[9]),'tx_errors':int(v[10]),'tx_drop':int(v[11])}) for l in open('/proc/net/dev').readlines()[2:] if ':' in l for i,d in [l.split(':',1)] for v in [d.split()] if len(v)>=12 and i.strip()!='lo'];print(json.dumps(rows if rows else []))"

linux.tcp_connections.description: TCP connection state counts: ESTABLISHED, TIME-WAIT, CLOSE-WAIT, and LISTEN sockets. Uses ss from iproute2.
linux.tcp_connections.command: python3 -c "import json,subprocess;out=subprocess.check_output(['ss','-an'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().split('\n');est=sum(1 for l in out if l.startswith('tcp') and 'ESTAB' in l);tw=sum(1 for l in out if l.startswith('tcp') and 'TIME-WAIT' in l);cw=sum(1 for l in out if l.startswith('tcp') and 'CLOSE-WAIT' in l);li=sum(1 for l in out if l.startswith('tcp') and 'LISTEN' in l);print(json.dumps({'established_connections':est,'time_wait_connections':tw,'close_wait_connections':cw,'listening_ports':li,'total_tcp_connections':est+tw+cw+li}))"

linux.services_health.description: systemd service health summary: total loaded units, actively running services, failed service count, and inactive service count. Requires systemd.
linux.services_health.command:
python3 -c "import json,subprocess
try:
    out=subprocess.check_output(['systemctl','list-units','--type=service','--all','--no-pager','--no-legend'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode()
    total=active=failed=inactive=0
    for l in out.strip().split('\n'):
        p=l.strip().lstrip(chr(9679)).split()
        if len(p)>=4:
            total+=1
            if p[2]=='active' and p[3]=='running':active+=1
            elif p[2]=='failed':failed+=1
            elif p[2]=='inactive':inactive+=1
    print(json.dumps({'total_units':total,'active_services':active,'failed_services':failed,'inactive_services':inactive}))
except:
    print(json.dumps({'total_units':0,'active_services':0,'failed_services':-1,'inactive_services':0}))"

linux.services_details.description: All loaded systemd services with load state, active state, sub-state, and description. Returns up to 100 rows. Use to investigate which services are failed or stopped.

linux.process_top_cpu.description: Top 20 processes by current CPU usage: username, PID, CPU%, memory%, VSZ, RSS, and command. Uses ps aux sorted by CPU descending.
linux.process_top_cpu.command: python3 -c "import json,subprocess;lines=subprocess.check_output(['ps','aux','--sort=-%cpu','--no-headers'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split('\n');rows=[];[rows.append({'user':p[0],'pid':int(p[1]),'cpu_pct':float(p[2]),'mem_pct':float(p[3]),'vsz_kb':int(p[4]),'rss_kb':int(p[5]),'stat':p[7],'command':p[10][:100] if len(p)>10 else ''}) for l in lines[:20] for p in [l.split(None,10)] if len(p)>=10 and p[1].isdigit()];print(json.dumps(rows))"

linux.process_top_memory.description: Top 20 processes by memory usage: username, PID, CPU%, memory%, VSZ, RSS, and command. Uses ps aux sorted by RSS descending.
linux.process_top_memory.command: python3 -c "import json,subprocess;lines=subprocess.check_output(['ps','aux','--sort=-%mem','--no-headers'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split('\n');rows=[];[rows.append({'user':p[0],'pid':int(p[1]),'cpu_pct':float(p[2]),'mem_pct':float(p[3]),'vsz_kb':int(p[4]),'rss_kb':int(p[5]),'stat':p[7],'command':p[10][:100] if len(p)>10 else ''}) for l in lines[:20] for p in [l.split(None,10)] if len(p)>=10 and p[1].isdigit()];print(json.dumps(rows))"

linux.performance_overview.description: System-wide process, thread, and zombie counts from ps. Useful for detecting thread leaks or zombie accumulation.
linux.performance_overview.command:
python3 -c "import json,subprocess;procs=subprocess.check_output(['ps','-e','--no-headers'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split('
');stats=subprocess.check_output(['ps','-eo','stat=','--no-headers'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split('
');threads=subprocess.check_output(['ps','-eLf','--no-headers'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split('
');zombies=sum(1 for s in stats if s.strip().startswith('Z'));print(json.dumps({'total_processes':len([p for p in procs if p.strip()]),'total_threads':len([t for t in threads if t.strip()]),'zombie_processes':zombies}))"

linux.syslog_errors.description: Error and critical event counts from journald in the past hour, split by severity. Requires journalctl and systemd-journal access.
linux.syslog_errors.command:
python3 -c "import json,subprocess,datetime
since=(datetime.datetime.now()-datetime.timedelta(hours=1)).strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
try:
    err=subprocess.run(['journalctl','--since',since,'--priority=err','--no-pager','--quiet'],capture_output=True,text=True,timeout=15)
    ec=len([l for l in err.stdout.strip().split('\n') if l.strip()]) if err.stdout.strip() else 0
    crit=subprocess.run(['journalctl','--since',since,'--priority=crit','--no-pager','--quiet'],capture_output=True,text=True,timeout=15)
    cc=len([l for l in crit.stdout.strip().split('\n') if l.strip()]) if crit.stdout.strip() else 0
    print(json.dumps({'error_count':ec,'critical_count':cc}))
except:
    print(json.dumps({'error_count':-1,'critical_count':-1}))"

linux.journal_errors.description: Most recent 50 error and critical journal entries from the past 2 hours. Returns rows with timestamp, unit, priority level, and message excerpt.
linux.journal_errors.command:
python3 -c "import json,subprocess,datetime
since=(datetime.datetime.now()-datetime.timedelta(hours=2)).strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
rows=[]
try:
    out=subprocess.run(['journalctl','--since',since,'--priority=err','--no-pager','--output=json','--lines=50'],capture_output=True,text=True,timeout=15)
    for l in out.stdout.strip().split('\n'):
        if l.strip():
            try:
                e=json.loads(l)
                ts=e.get('__REALTIME_TIMESTAMP','')
                if ts:
                    import datetime as dt
                    ts=dt.datetime.fromtimestamp(int(ts)/1e6).strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
                rows.append({'time':ts,'unit':e.get('_SYSTEMD_UNIT',e.get('SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER','')),'priority':e.get('PRIORITY',''),'message':str(e.get('MESSAGE',''))[:200]})
            except:pass
except:pass
print(json.dumps(rows[:50] if rows else [{'time':'','unit':'system','priority':'','message':'No errors found or journald unavailable'}]))"

linux.failed_logins.description: Failed SSH login attempt count in the past hour, from journald (sshd unit) or /var/log/auth.log fallback. Counts Failed password, Invalid user, and authentication failure events.

linux.local_users.description: Local user account summary: total accounts, regular users (UID 1000-65533), root/UID-0 accounts, and sudo/wheel group member counts.
linux.local_users.command:
python3 -c "import json,subprocess
users=[l.split(':') for l in open('/etc/passwd').read().strip().split('\n') if ':' in l and not l.startswith('#')]
regular=[u for u in users if len(u)>=3 and u[2].isdigit() and 1000<=int(u[2])<65534]
root_count=sum(1 for u in users if len(u)>=3 and u[2]=='0')
def grp(g):
    try:
        m=subprocess.check_output(['getent','group',g],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split(':')[-1].strip()
        return len(m.split(',')) if m else 0
    except:
        return 0
print(json.dumps({'total_users':len(users),'regular_users':len(regular),'root_uid0_count':root_count,'sudo_group_members':grp('sudo'),'wheel_group_members':grp('wheel')}))"

linux.open_ports.description: Count of listening TCP and UDP ports using ss. High counts may indicate unintended services or misconfigured applications.
linux.open_ports.command: python3 -c "import json,subprocess;tcp=subprocess.check_output(['ss','-tlnH'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split('\n');udp=subprocess.check_output(['ss','-ulnH'],stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL).decode().strip().split('\n');tc=len([l for l in tcp if l.strip()]);uc=len([l for l in udp if l.strip()]);print(json.dumps({'listening_tcp_ports':tc,'listening_udp_ports':uc,'total_listening_ports':tc+uc}))"

linux.system_info.description: OS name, distribution ID, version, codename, kernel version, architecture, hostname, total memory in GB, CPU core count, and uptime in days. Reads /etc/os-release, /proc, and uname.

linux_cpu_usage_warning.title: CPU Usage High (Warning)
linux_cpu_usage_warning.recommendation: Review running processes. Use linux.process_top_cpu to identify high-CPU consumers. Check for runaway cron jobs or application loops.

linux_cpu_usage_critical.title: CPU Usage Critical (90%+)
linux_cpu_usage_critical.recommendation: Server is CPU-saturated. Identify and terminate runaway processes immediately. Consider migrating workloads or adding CPU capacity.

linux_load_avg_warning.title: Load Average High (Warning)
linux_load_avg_warning.recommendation: 1-minute load average exceeds 8. A load above the number of CPU cores indicates queueing. Check cpu_cores and review the top processes for CPU and I/O bottlenecks.

linux_load_avg_critical.title: Load Average Critical (>16)
linux_load_avg_critical.recommendation: Sustained load average over 16 indicates severe CPU or I/O saturation. Identify the dominant consumers immediately. The server may become unresponsive.

linux_memory_usage_warning.title: Memory Usage High (Warning)
linux_memory_usage_warning.recommendation: Memory usage exceeds 80%. Review top memory consumers with linux.process_top_memory. Check for memory leaks in long-running processes.

linux_memory_usage_critical.title: Memory Usage Critical (90%+)
linux_memory_usage_critical.recommendation: Memory is nearly exhausted. The kernel will invoke the OOM killer, which can terminate critical processes. Identify and restart memory-leaking services immediately.

linux_swap_usage_warning.title: Swap Usage High (Warning)
linux_swap_usage_warning.recommendation: More than 50% of swap is in use, indicating memory pressure. Active swapping severely degrades performance. Identify memory-heavy processes and consider adding RAM.

linux_swap_usage_critical.title: Swap Usage Critical (80%+)
linux_swap_usage_critical.recommendation: Swap is nearly exhausted. The system is critically low on virtual memory. Risk of OOM kills and process crashes is high. Immediately free memory or expand swap space.

linux_disk_space_warning.title: Disk Space Usage High (Warning)
linux_disk_space_warning.recommendation: At least one filesystem is over 80% full. Review linux.disk_details to identify which mount. Run du -sh /* to find large directories. Archive or delete old logs.

linux_disk_space_critical.title: Disk Space Usage Critical (90%+)
linux_disk_space_critical.recommendation: A filesystem is over 90% full. Linux services will fail when disk space is exhausted. Immediately free space, expand the volume, or move data to another filesystem.

linux_network_receive_errors_warning.title: Network Receive Errors Detected
linux_network_receive_errors_warning.recommendation: Network adapter receive errors are accumulating. Check cable connections, NIC driver, and duplex/speed settings. Review ethtool -S <iface> for detailed NIC counters.

linux_network_receive_errors_critical.title: Network Receive Errors Critical
linux_network_receive_errors_critical.recommendation: High network error rate indicates a hardware or driver problem. Replace the cable, check the switch port, or update the NIC driver. This causes packet loss affecting all services.

linux_services_failed.title: systemd Service Failed
linux_services_failed.recommendation: One or more systemd services are in the failed state. Run 'systemctl --failed' to list them and 'journalctl -u <service>' to investigate the failure reason.

linux_services_many_failed.title: Many systemd Services Failed
linux_services_many_failed.recommendation: More than 5 systemd services are failed. This may indicate a system-wide issue such as a failed boot, resource exhaustion, or a dependency failure cascade. Review journald immediately.

linux_zombie_processes_high.title: Zombie Processes Accumulating
linux_zombie_processes_high.recommendation: More than 5 zombie processes are present. Zombies accumulate when a parent process does not call wait(). Find the parent PID and investigate or restart it. Zombies consume PID table space.

linux_syslog_errors_warning.title: System Log Error Rate High
linux_syslog_errors_warning.recommendation: More than 50 error-level journal entries in the past hour. Review linux.journal_errors to identify the dominant sources. Common causes: application crashes, kernel warnings, or disk errors.

linux_syslog_errors_critical.title: System Log Error Rate Critical
linux_syslog_errors_critical.recommendation: Over 200 error log entries in the past hour indicates a systemic problem. Review journalctl -p err -n 100 immediately to identify the dominant source. Consider a hardware failure or runaway service.

linux_critical_events_detected.title: Critical-Level Events in System Journal
linux_critical_events_detected.recommendation: Critical-level events (priority 2) have been recorded in journald. These represent kernel panics, hardware errors, or severe application faults. Investigate immediately with 'journalctl -p crit'.

linux_failed_logins_warning.title: Failed SSH Login Attempts Elevated
linux_failed_logins_warning.recommendation: More than 20 failed SSH login attempts in the past hour. May indicate brute-force attempts. Review auth.log or journalctl -u sshd for source IPs. Consider fail2ban or firewall rules.

linux_failed_logins_critical.title: Failed SSH Logins Critical — Possible Brute-Force
linux_failed_logins_critical.recommendation: Over 100 failed SSH logins in an hour is a strong brute-force indicator. Block the source IP at the firewall immediately, enable fail2ban, and verify no accounts have been compromised.

linux_sudo_users_high.title: Excessive sudo/wheel Group Members
linux_sudo_users_high.recommendation: More than 5 users are in the sudo group. Follow the principle of least privilege. Remove unnecessary sudo memberships and use dedicated service accounts with minimal permissions.

linux_listening_ports_high.title: High Number of Listening Ports
linux_listening_ports_high.recommendation: More than 30 ports are listening for connections. Review ss -tlnp and ss -ulnp to identify unexpected listeners. Disable or firewall services that should not be exposed.

hints: What is the current CPU utilization trend and are there any processes causing spikes?
Is the load average consistently above the number of CPU cores? What is causing it?
Which processes are consuming the most CPU time right now?
Are there any zombie processes accumulating? What is their parent process?
Should this server's workload be redistributed to other hosts?
Is the memory usage within acceptable bounds, or is the server under memory pressure?
Are there signs of swap usage that indicate RAM exhaustion?
Which processes are consuming the most memory and could any be restarted?
Are any filesystems approaching capacity? Which one needs attention first?
Is there a trend of disk space being consumed faster than expected?
Are any systemd services in the failed state? What is the failure reason?
What is the server uptime and were there any recent unexpected reboots?
Are there services that have failed repeatedly and should be investigated?
Is the TCP connection profile normal or are there signs of connection leaks?
Are there any critical or error events in the system journal that need immediate attention?
What are the dominant services generating the most error log entries?
Are there signs of a brute-force SSH attack or unusual failed login patterns?
Are the sudo group members appropriate, or should any be removed?
Are there unexpectedly listening ports that should be firewalled?
Provide an executive summary of this Linux server's overall health.
What are the top 3 most urgent issues to address on this server right now?
What proactive maintenance tasks would improve the stability of this server over the next 30 days?
Based on the findings and metrics, what is the overall risk level for this server?

metrics.name: cpu_usage_percent
metrics.label: CPU Usage
metrics.hint: CPU utilization over the past second — all cores combined

metrics.name: load_avg_1
metrics.label: Load Avg (1m)
metrics.hint: 1-minute load average; above cpu_cores indicates saturation

metrics.name: load_avg_5
metrics.label: Load Avg (5m)
metrics.hint: 5-minute load average — more stable indicator of sustained load

metrics.name: load_avg_15
metrics.label: Load Avg (15m)
metrics.hint: 15-minute load average — trending indicator

metrics.name: cpu_cores
metrics.label: CPU Cores
metrics.hint: Logical CPU count from /proc/cpuinfo

metrics.name: uptime_seconds
metrics.label: Uptime
metrics.hint: Seconds since last boot; low value indicates a recent reboot

metrics.name: memory_used_percent
metrics.label: Memory Used
metrics.hint: Physical RAM in use as a percentage (total minus available)

metrics.name: memory_available_bytes
metrics.label: Available Memory
metrics.hint: Memory available for new allocations including reclaimable cache

metrics.name: memory_total_bytes
metrics.label: Total Memory
metrics.hint: Total installed physical RAM

metrics.name: cached_bytes
metrics.label: Page Cache
metrics.hint: Reclaimable page cache; Linux uses free RAM for caching

metrics.name: swap_used_percent
metrics.label: Swap Used
metrics.hint: Swap in use as %; active swap use indicates memory pressure

metrics.name: swap_total_bytes
metrics.label: Total Swap
metrics.hint: Total swap space configured

metrics.name: max_disk_used_pct
metrics.label: Max Disk Used
metrics.hint: Highest used % across all real filesystems

metrics.name: total_disk_bytes
metrics.label: Total Disk
metrics.hint: Combined capacity of all mounted filesystems

metrics.name: free_disk_bytes
metrics.label: Free Disk
metrics.hint: Combined free space across all real filesystems

metrics.name: volume_count
metrics.label: Volume Count
metrics.hint: Number of mounted real filesystems

metrics.name: disk_read_bytes_per_sec
metrics.label: Disk Read B/s
metrics.hint: Block device read throughput per second

metrics.name: disk_write_bytes_per_sec
metrics.label: Disk Write B/s
metrics.hint: Block device write throughput per second

metrics.name: read_iops
metrics.label: Read IOPS
metrics.hint: Read operations per second across all block devices

metrics.name: write_iops
metrics.label: Write IOPS
metrics.hint: Write operations per second across all block devices

metrics.name: bytes_received_per_sec
metrics.label: Bytes In/s
metrics.hint: Total inbound network throughput across all non-loopback interfaces

metrics.name: bytes_sent_per_sec
metrics.label: Bytes Out/s
metrics.hint: Total outbound network throughput

metrics.name: total_receive_errors
metrics.label: Receive Errors
metrics.hint: Cumulative inbound packet errors — non-zero warrants NIC investigation

metrics.name: total_send_errors
metrics.label: Send Errors
metrics.hint: Cumulative outbound packet errors

metrics.name: established_connections
metrics.label: Established Connections
metrics.hint: TCP connections in ESTABLISHED state

metrics.name: time_wait_connections
metrics.label: TIME_WAIT Connections
metrics.hint: TCP connections in TIME_WAIT; high count may indicate port exhaustion

metrics.name: listening_ports
metrics.label: Listening Ports
metrics.hint: TCP ports in LISTEN state

metrics.name: failed_services
metrics.label: Failed Services
metrics.hint: Count of systemd services in failed state — non-zero needs investigation

metrics.name: active_services
metrics.label: Active Services
metrics.hint: systemd services currently in active/running state

metrics.name: total_units
metrics.label: Total Units
metrics.hint: Total loaded systemd service units

metrics.name: total_processes
metrics.label: Total Processes
metrics.hint: All running processes on the system

metrics.name: total_threads
metrics.label: Total Threads
metrics.hint: All threads; rising value may indicate a thread leak

metrics.name: zombie_processes
metrics.label: Zombie Processes
metrics.hint: Processes in Z state waiting for parent to call wait()

metrics.name: error_count
metrics.label: Journal Errors
metrics.hint: Error-priority events in journald in the last collection window

metrics.name: critical_count
metrics.label: Critical Events
metrics.hint: Critical-priority events — kernel panics, hardware faults, severe crashes

metrics.name: failed_login_count
metrics.label: Failed SSH Logins/hr
metrics.hint: Failed SSH authentication attempts; > 20/hr indicates brute-force

metrics.name: sudo_group_members
metrics.label: Sudo Group Members
metrics.hint: Users in the sudo group; > 5 is flagged as a security risk

metrics.name: root_uid0_count
metrics.label: Root UID-0 Accounts
metrics.hint: Accounts with UID 0 (root equivalent); should be exactly 1

metrics.name: regular_users
metrics.label: Regular Users
metrics.hint: Local user accounts with UID 1000-65533

metrics.name: total_listening_ports
metrics.label: Listening Ports (Total)
metrics.hint: Combined TCP+UDP listening ports; high count warrants review

metrics.name: listening_tcp_ports
metrics.label: TCP Listening Ports
metrics.hint: TCP ports accepting connections
